rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ELSABET T. JONES IRREVOCABLE DECEMBER 13, 2011 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Seal Private Financial Information
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Motion to Seal Private Financial Information, brought by beneficiary Elsabet Jones. Beginning April 2015, Ms. Jones' parents, in their capacities as co-trustees, engaged in a dispute over fiduciary control of the trust. On June 15, 2015, a Guardian Ad Litem was appointed to represent the then-minor Elsabet's beneficiary interests. The court entered Order Granting Alternative Relief on May 8, 2015; and entered Order to Supplement May 8, 2015 Order Granting Alternative Relief on February 16, 2016, on stipulated request of parties. After preparation for trial, mediation efforts, and stipulated continuances of trial, the co-trustee parties reached a settlement as of April 2017. Petitioner Barbara Brooks filed Dismissal with Prejudice on January 26, 2018. Beneficiary Elsabet Jones, now an adult, recently discovered the documents and pleadings filed in the above action did not redact or seal any of the then-current financial information and asset holdings of the Elsabet T. Jones Irrevocable Trust II (Decl. of E. Jones at par. 6). This Motion seeks to seal specific financial information - dollar amounts in trust accounts and the value of corporate shares held by the trust - contained in eleven specified filings in this action, so as to protect Ms. Jones' financial privacy interests as the sole beneficiary of the trust. Procedurally, the court notes that this request probably should have been filed by the trustee, to protect and preserve trust property, on behalf of the sole beneficiary's interest (see, e.g., Prob. C. section 16006, 16012, 16249). The papers and declarations indicate that the beneficiary is acting with the trustee's awareness and support of this motion, however, the beneficiary is the sole income beneficiary of the trust, and there are no objections to the motion (see also, Prob. C. section 16249(b), 16500, 16501). The court waives any procedural defects in bringing this motion to seal. The court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (CRC 2.550(d).) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.) [Part 1 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ELSABET T. JONES IRREVOCABLE DECEMBER 13, 2011 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion To Seal Private Financial Information (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The public's right of access to court records is fundamental to our constitutional system. (See NBC Subsidiary, supra, 20 Cal.4th 1216-1217 [First Amendment principles require that substantive courtroom proceedings in ordinary civil cases are presumptively open]; Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(c) [court records presumed to be open].) Even absent general interest in a particular case, open access ensures that the press and private citizens can inform themselves regarding particular disputes or community events. Also, critically, open access to court records promotes judicial accountability. "Litigants certainly anticipate, upon submitting their disputes for resolution in a public court, before a state-appointed or publicly elected judge, that the proceedings in their case will be adjudicated in public." (NBC Subsidiary, supra, 20 Cal.4th at p. 1211.) The right to access is heightened in probate proceedings where broad notice and opportunity to object is fundamental. (See, e.g., Estate of Stevenson (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 1074, 1087-88 ["Notice is the cornerstone of estate proceedings. [Citations.]"].) Notice is lacking when the documents and information submitted for judicial review are withheld from interested parties, effectively removing them from the decision-making process. Typically, a probate proceeding will require an open discussion of the information presented in the filings - including financial information, such as the trust assets to be accounted for, or the amounts of alleged misused or misappropriated trust funds. The open discussion of that information would not be possible if the information is under seal. The facts before the court on this motion to seal are unusual, however, as the motion comes years after the filings were entered in the public record, and years after the legal dispute was settled. Notice to interested parties, opportunity to be heard, and the court's judicial review and decision-making processes, were all done with the original unredacted documents. Ms. Jones, as the sole beneficiary, has shown she has an overriding interest in keeping the financial facts of her trust private. (Decl. of E. Jones at par. 4, 6; Decl. of Brooks at par. 1, 2; Decl. of H. Jones at par. 1, 2.) This interest overcomes the public interest in the specific dollar amounts of assets held in the trust or held in corporate entities in which the trust holds an interest, listed in the specified documents. Sealing those dollar amounts does not obscure the court's decision-making process or the co-trustees' legal disputes in this matter. The initial Petition sought to remove the co-trustee, suspend his powers as co-trustee, appoint a private professional fiduciary, and freeze the trust accounts until appointment of a successor trustee. The information submitted, and now sought to be sealed, was not reviewed in connection with an accounting or fee request. The specific dollar amounts listed in the filed documents were not determinative of the requested relief, and are not included in the court's orders. There is no anticipated need for the court to review trust accountings or fee requests in the future that would require an open discussion of the information now sought to be sealed. The trust is not under court supervision, and there are no pending petitions or applications. Further, the motion has shown a substantial probability that the interest in keeping these financial details of the trust private will be prejudiced if the specified information is not sealed. This is demonstrated by Ms. Jones' recent encounter with a person demanding money from her, and her subsequent discovery that the financial details of a trust titled in her name, listing her as the sole beneficiary, has been on the public record for the past eight years (Decl. of E. Jones at par. 6). [Part 2 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ELSABET T. JONES IRREVOCABLE DECEMBER 13, 2011 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion To Seal Private Financial Information (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] The requested sealing is sufficiently tailored, encompassing only the dollar amounts of trust assets listed in specified documents. There is no less restrictive means by which to protect the overriding interest in keeping these financial details private. An order sealing the record must direct the sealing of only those documents and pages, or, if reasonably practicable, portions of those documents and pages, that contain the material that needs to be placed under seal. All other portions of each document or page must be included in the public file. (CRC 2.550(e).) Motion to Seal Private Financial Information is GRANTED, as to the dollar amounts listed in the following documents, at the specified page and line numbers, or otherwise specified pages: [list of documents, with page/line number, or identification of attachment page(s), to be supplied by moving party]. In order to effectuate this sealing order while otherwise preserving the right to access the public record, the moving party is ORDERED to submit for filing redacted versions of these documents, with redactions at the specified page and line numbers, or otherwise specified pages. These initially-filed, unredacted documents are ORDERED SEALED, following the moving party completing filing the redacted versions of these documents. The moving party is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, as published above, by the time set for this hearing, and also lodge a separate document identifying the document, page and line number for redaction. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D). To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 3 of 3]
